1.exposition: Chapter 1 ended with a catalog of sins so lurid that the respectable reader could only nod along in agreement — and that nod is the trap Paul now springs. You have no excuse, O man, every one of you who judges, for in condemning another you sentence yourself, since you do the same things. The moralist's applause for the indictment of others is itself a confession. From there the principles fall in order: God judges according to truth, not appearance; his patience is a summons to repentance, not proof that sin does not matter (v.4); the hard heart stores up wrath; God will render to each according to his works; and he shows no partiality — to the Jew first and also to the Greek. This is the great leveler, and it opens the letter's hardest knot: a judgment "according to works" standing beside a gospel of justification by faith.
1.original_language: The first word of the verdict is anapologetos ("without defense, inexcusable") — the very word Paul used of the idolatrous Gentile in 1:20; the judge now stands in the dock he built. God's chrestotes ("kindness," v.4) is meant to lead to metanoia — a change of mind, a turning. And the sinner does not merely incur wrath but thesaurizeis ("treasures it up," v.5) — the language of hoarding, but the hoard is judgment. "Render according to his works" (v.6) reaches back to the Old Testament refrain of Psalm 62:12 and Proverbs 24:12.
1.historical_context: Paul shifts into diatribe, the classroom style in which a teacher rounds on an imagined objector ("O man," v.1) and answers the retort before it is spoken. The judge's identity is deliberately left open — moralizing Gentile or Torah-confident Jew — and only sharpens into the Jew by v.17. The logic of self-conviction has deep prophetic roots, like Nathan's "You are the man!" to David (2 Samuel 12:7). The three terms for God's patience (v.4) correct a Second-Temple assumption that God's delay of judgment on Israel proved that its sin did not finally count.
1.christ: The certain day of wrath, when God's righteous judgment will be revealed, is the very judgment from which only Christ delivers; his intercession is what stands between the believer and that day (8:34). The principle that God repays each according to his works (v.6) is not abolished by the gospel but answered at the cross, where Christ bears the repayment wrath requires for all united to him. And the kindness "meant to lead you to repentance" (v.4) finds its fullest form in the Son given for sinners — the patience of God made flesh.

verses.note: You have no excuse, O man, every one of you who judges — anapologetos, the word already spoken over the Gentile in 1:20. reformed Gill notes some take the judge as the moralizing Gentile (a Socrates or Seneca) who reproved vice yet practiced it, others as the Jew who despised the Gentiles; either way he is inexcusable, condemned out of his own mouth. grammatical-historical Barnes presses the appeal to conscience: in passing sentence on another's sin, the man ratifies the very standard that convicts himself.

We know that the judgment of God rightly falls on those who do such things — kata aletheian, according to truth. reformed Gill takes this of the judgment to come — certain, universal, definitive, carried on by the omniscient God — not the partial verdicts of this life. grammatical-historical Barnes hears in "we know" the admitted conscience of mankind that God will punish such crimes. Where the hypocrite judges by appearance, God searches the real state of things: "shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?" (Genesis 18:25).

Do you suppose you will escape the judgment of God? grammatical-historical Barnes frames it as an argument from the lesser to the greater: if the hypocrite's own imperfect sense of justice condemns the sin, how much more the holy God — and how shall he escape who has already delivered the same sentence? The presumption of impunity is self-deception; the judge is undone by his own verdict, "out of your own mouth" (Luke 19:22).

Do you presume on the riches of his kindness and forbearance and patience? reformed Gill answers the objection drawn from prosperity — the sinner's flourishing was never his innocence but the riches of God's forbearance. wesleyan-arminian Clarke fixes on the design: the goodness of God leads to repentance, and to harden under it is to despise the very kindness sent to save. The delay is space to turn, for "the longsuffering of our Lord is salvation" (2 Peter 3:9).

But by your hard and impenitent heart you store up wrath for yourself. reformed Gill distinguishes a natural hardness in every son of Adam, an acquired hardness deepened by sinning, and a judicial hardness God gives the sinner over to; the impenitent heart cannot of itself repent. The image is dreadful: as a man hoards treasure, the hard heart hoards wrath — every unrepented sin added to the store — against the day when the righteous judgment is unveiled (cf. Revelation 6:17).

He will render to each one according to his works (Psalm 62:12) — and here the witnesses divide honestly. reformed Gill renders to the wicked the demerit of their sins and to the good eternal life, but insists the believer's works have no merit; they are the evidence and fruit of faith, not its ground — "faith without works is dead" (James 2:26), yet "the free gift of God is eternal life" (6:23). wesleyan-arminian Clarke reads the recompense to the well-doer more straightforwardly: God genuinely crowns the obedient life. Both deny raw merit; they differ over whether the next verse's "well-doer" is simply the regenerate whose works evidence faith, or every person who by grace perseveres and is truly rewarded.

To those who by patience in well-doing seek glory and honor and immortality, eternal life. reformed Gill limits these carefully — not the best-moralized Gentile (who sought applause), nor the pharisaical Jew (who sought righteousness by works), nor any unregenerate man, but only those in whom grace is implanted, whose perseverance is grace's own fruit issuing in life. The wesleyan-arminian ear hears instead a real, grace-enabled condition set genuinely before all. Either way it is no fitful zeal but a patient continuance — "be faithful unto death" (Revelation 2:10), the heart set on things above (Colossians 3:1).

But for the self-seeking who do not obey the truth but obey unrighteousness, wrath and fury. reformed Gill hears the quarrelsome who contend for victory, not truth; grammatical-historical Barnes reads the word (eritheia, faction) as a disposition toward God himself — rebellious, contending with the Almighty, resisting his claims. The verse is the dark mirror of v.7: two hearts, two masters, two ends — the one obeying the truth, the other serving sin.

Tribulation and distress for every human being who does evil, the Jew first and also the Greek. reformed Gill takes these words for the second death, the torments of hell; grammatical-historical Barnes distinguishes "tribulation" (the being pressed down by punishment) from "anguish" (the soul's extreme distress). The "Jew first" is no favoritism reversed but accountability weighted by privilege — "to whom much is given, of him much will be required" (Luke 12:48).

But glory and honor and peace for everyone who does good, the Jew first and also the Greek. reformed Gill reads the triad as the everlasting happiness of the saints — a crown of glory, an honor exceeding the greatest potentate, a peace that passes understanding. wesleyan-arminian Clarke notes the balance: where the impenitent meet indignation, every man who works good — who lives in obedience — receives glory and honor. The blessing, like the doom of v.9, falls in order yet without partiality.

For God shows no partiality — prosopolempsia, no "receiving of the face." reformed Gill spells out what will not count on that day: not nation, parentage, age, sex, or sect, nor conformity to any external — only whether a man is righteous or a sinner. This single line frames the whole section: the same word that levels the Gentile levels the Jew (cf. Acts 10:34) — a warning to the privileged, and a door thrown open to the outsider.

witnesses.point: God renders to each according to his works by the truth of the case; the believer's works are the evidence and fruit of grace, never its merit, so the wicked are condemned and the righteous rewarded with no ground of desert.

The judge condemns himself: every appeal to the standard by which he faults another is a sentence pronounced over his own head, and God's judgment is that human sense of justice made perfect and impartial.
God's goodness is designed to lead to repentance, and glory and honor genuinely crown the obedient life; to harden under his forbearance is to despise the very riches meant to save.

external.name: Douglas J. Moo
external.point: Paul states judgment according to works as a real and universal biblical standard, not a mere hypothesis; the section closes every exit so that the verdict of Romans 3:20 — no one justified by works of the law — arrives as the only honest conclusion, driving the reader toward the righteousness of God by faith.

1.application: God's patience is not the same as God's approval. Every unhurried day is either a summons answered or wrath quietly stored. The kindest thing the delay can mean is that there is still time to turn.

12.exposition: Paul now presses the impartiality of judgment into the question of the law itself. Those who sinned without the law will perish without it, and those who sinned under the law will be judged by it — each measured by the light he actually had. For it is not the hearers of the law who are righteous, but the doers who will be justified. Even Gentiles who never received Sinai may do by nature what the law requires, showing its work written on their hearts, conscience accusing or excusing them. And all of it will be settled on the day when God judges the secrets of men by Christ Jesus. Possessing the law shelters no one; doing it is what counts — and the argument moving toward chapter 3 is that no one does it fully.
12.original_language: The Gentiles who follow their inward sense are heautois eisin nomos — "a law to themselves" (v.14), not autonomous but supplied with a standard from within. Their syneidesis ("conscience," v.15) presides over a courtroom of thoughts, kategorounton or apologoumenon — accusing or defending. And in v.13 the decisive verb of the whole letter sounds for the first time: dikaiothesontai, "they will be justified" — the word Paul will spend chapters unfolding.
12.historical_context: Paul calibrates one standard to differing knowledge — Gentiles judged apart from a law they never received, Jews by the law they did — a principle resonant with the Torah's own distinction between inadvertent and deliberate sin (Numbers 15:27–31). "The doers of the law will be justified" (v.13) counters a Second-Temple confidence that possessing and studying Torah was itself meritorious (cf. James 1:22). And Gentiles doing "by nature" what the law requires engages the Stoic notion of a moral order accessible to reason — without ever claiming that Gentiles are naturally good.
12.christ: The section's climax places Christ at the center of the final assize: God judges the secrets of men by Christ Jesus, the appointed judge of all (Acts 17:31), and the standard is the gospel Paul preaches. Since neither Gentile conscience nor Jewish Torah actually produces the righteousness God requires (v.13), both roads converge on the one thing only Christ provides (3:21–24). The Lord who reads the secret heart is the same Lord whose death opens the only way to stand acquitted before it.

verses.note: All who have sinned without the law will also perish without the law. reformed Gill explains: the Gentiles were without the written law of Moses, yet not without the law of nature in their breasts; sinning against the God and the light of nature, they perish justly. grammatical-historical Barnes marks this as the very principle by which God is no respecter of persons — the Gentile judged by nature, the Jew by the law, and both alike shut up under guilt (cf. 3:19).

Not the hearers of the law are righteous before God, but the doers of the law will be justified. reformed Gill notes the sting for the Jew: the bare hearing of the law every sabbath, without doing, never made anyone righteous. grammatical-historical Barnes states the principle plainly — if justification is sought by law, only the doers can attain it. Read as the law's own standard, it condemns rather than saves: since none perfectly does it (3:20, "by works of the law no flesh is justified"), the door it opens is one no sinner can walk through, and it drives all to Christ.

When Gentiles who do not have the law do by nature the things of the law, they are a law to themselves. reformed Gill owns the Gentiles lacked the written law, yet had a law they in part obeyed. Here the witnesses genuinely differ on whom Paul means: catholic Augustine took these law-doers to be Gentile Christians who keep the law by grace, whereas grammatical-historical Barnes and Gill read the natural conscience of pagans — enough moral knowledge to make the heathen accountable, though not to save.

They show the work of the law written on their hearts, their conscience also bearing witness, their thoughts accusing or excusing them. reformed Gill distinguishes this from the tables of stone: the Gentiles have the substance of the law in the mind — the moral law inscribed on Adam's heart at creation, defaced at the fall, its remnants retained. The conscience is the inward monitor, holding its own trial. This natural writing must not be confused with the new-covenant heart-writing promised in Jeremiah 31:33.

On the day when God judges the secrets of men by Christ Jesus, according to my gospel. reformed Gill connects this back to v.12, reading verses 13–15 as a parenthesis; the "day" is fixed and its judgment clear. grammatical-historical Barnes sums the whole: God will judge all in righteousness, the Jew by the law and the heathen by conscience. Nothing is hidden — the secrets, the very thoughts, are laid bare before Christ the Judge (John 5:22), a reckoning Paul counts part of his gospel.

witnesses.point: Each is judged by the light he actually possessed — the Gentile by the law of nature written on the heart, the Jew by the written law — so that hearing without doing justifies no one and every mouth is stopped.

The parenthesis on conscience proves God to be no respecter of persons: he will judge the heathen by the light of nature and the Jew by the law, and both fall short of the standard by which they are measured.
Read the Gentiles who "do by nature the things of the law" as Gentile believers, in whom grace writes the law upon the heart — not merely pagans following an unaided conscience.

external.name: C.E.B. Cranfield
external.point: Gives serious weight to reading the law-doing Gentiles of vv.14–15 as Gentile Christians, the law "written on their hearts" echoing the new-covenant promise of Jeremiah 31, while granting the accountability reading; on either view the verse insists that no one is left wholly without moral light.

12.application: Familiarity with Scripture is not the same as obedience to it. The most exposed place before God is not ignorance but knowledge unlived — and a day is coming when even the secrets are read aloud.

17.exposition: Now the interlocutor is named. If you call yourself a Jew and rely on the law and boast in God, confident that you are a guide to the blind, a light to those in darkness — then comes the searching turn: you who teach others, do you not teach yourself? The one who preaches against stealing steals; who forbids adultery commits it; who abhors idols robs temples. So by breaking the law you dishonor God, and the outcome is Scripture's own verdict: the name of God is blasphemed among the Gentiles because of you. The catalog of privileges Paul rehearses in verses 17–20 is real and precious; but recited over a life that contradicts it, the boast becomes an indictment. Possessing and teaching the law has not made Israel righteous — it has exposed its hypocrisy.
17.original_language: The diatribe finally names its target: Ioudaios ("Jew"), the honored name bound to Judah. Twice Paul uses kauchasai — "you boast" (v.17, v.23) — a key verb of the letter that he will later strip from every human claim (cf. 3:27). And in v.20 the Jew has the morphosis ("outward form, framework") of knowledge and truth in the law — the shape of it, Paul implies, without the power.
17.historical_context: "If you call yourself a Jew" (v.17) addresses the proud religious identity of the diaspora, where reliance on the law and boasting in God were genuine and treasured marks of covenant privilege (Deuteronomy 4:8). The roles claimed — guide, light, instructor, teacher (vv.19–20) — echo Israel's true vocation to be a light to the nations (Isaiah 49:6), here turned to accusation. The closing citation (v.24) draws on Isaiah 52:5 and Ezekiel 36:20–23, where Israel's disgrace made the nations scorn its God.
17.christ: By showing that even Israel — entrusted with the law and called to be the world's light — has failed and dishonored God (vv.23–24), Paul brings the covenant people into the same universal plight and so the same universal need of a Savior. The vocation Israel could not fulfill, to be a light to the nations (Isaiah 49:6), is taken up and accomplished by the true Israel, Christ, in whom God's name is hallowed rather than blasphemed. The failure of the law-bearing people points forward to the One who keeps the law perfectly and bears its curse for the lawbreaker.

verses.note: You call yourself a Jew and rely on the law and boast in God. eastern Chrysostom notes that Paul sets down the glories of the Jews — beginning with the very name, then of great majesty (as "Christian" is now) — yet says "you are called a Jew," not "you are one," and "you boast in God" as one loved and honored above all men; "here he seems to me to be gently mocking" the boast. To "rely on the law" is the false rest — trusting the mere possession of it (contrast v.13).

You know his will and approve what is excellent, being instructed from the law. reformed Gill identifies "his will" as God's declared will in the law — a real privilege other peoples lacked — but warns that bare knowledge avails nothing, for a man may know his Lord's will and not do it (Luke 12:47; James 4:17). grammatical-historical Barnes notes the knowledge came from the Scriptures. Light not walked in only deepens the guilt.

You are confident that you are a guide to the blind, a light to those in darkness. grammatical-historical Barnes observes that "confident" denotes the Jew's full assurance of superiority in knowledge — and remarks on the fact that the Jews were no speculative infidels but held the firmest conviction that God was the Author of their institutions. The privilege was genuine; the pride would be exposed by a teacher who could not teach himself (v.21).

An instructor of the foolish, a teacher of children, having in the law the embodiment of knowledge and truth. reformed Gill notes that the Jewish doctors were styled "instructors" of the simple who knew not the law. grammatical-historical Barnes takes "the foolish" as those void of understanding, "children" as figurative. The telling word is morphosis — the Jew has the form of knowledge and truth: the framework without the reality, the letter without the spirit (v.29), a shape of godliness without its power (cf. 2 Timothy 3:5).

You who teach others, do you not teach yourself? You who preach against stealing, do you steal? reformed Gill reads these not as open questions but as assertions of fact: the teachers taught others and not themselves, blind leaders of the blind, ignorant of the law's spirituality. The gap between profession and practice is the self-condemnation of v.1 come home — "physician, heal yourself" (Luke 4:23).

You who say one must not commit adultery, do you commit adultery? You who abhor idols, do you rob temples? reformed Gill takes the adultery literally; grammatical-historical Barnes notes there is no doubt the sin was found among the Jews — the Talmud itself accuses celebrated rabbis — and that, abhorring idols, they yet committed sacrilege, whether robbing temples or robbing God in withheld tithes (Malachi 3:8). The hypocrisy reaches even the gravest sins the Jew professed to condemn.

You who boast in the law dishonor God by breaking the law. grammatical-historical Barnes turns the boast into evidence: to glory in the law is to confess its excellence and its claim from God; but since actions are the true test of a man's real opinions, the breaking of it dishonored God more than the boasting ever honored him. reformed Gill adds that the Jew boasted even of keeping the law to perfection — the louder the boast, the deeper the contradiction.

The name of God is blasphemed among the Gentiles because of you, as it is written. reformed Gill notes it is God's very being, perfections, law, and worship that are evil spoken of. grammatical-historical Barnes traces the cause: the pagan world, seeing a religion with no power to purify its own professors, despised it — and the reproach terminated on its Author. Paul grounds the charge in the prophets (Isaiah 52:5; Ezekiel 36:20–23) — the professing people's sin bringing shame on the holy name (cf. 2 Peter 2:2).

witnesses.point: Paul rehearses the honored name "Jew" and the boast in God only to mock them gently — the title without the reality, the boast his own conduct will overturn.

Actions are the true test of a man's real opinions: by boasting in the law the Jew proclaimed it divine, and by breaking it dishonored God more than his boast ever honored him, so that the pagans blasphemed.
The teacher's questions are matters of fact — he taught others but not himself, a blind leader of the blind, whose knowledge of God's will only aggravated the guilt of not doing it.

external.name: James D.G. Dunn
external.point: Reads the "boast" of vv.17 and 23 as covenantal confidence in Israel's privileged status and possession of the law — the badges of national identity; Paul's charge is that reliance on these markers, without the obedience they were meant to seal, turns Israel's vocation to be a light into the very occasion of God's name being scorned (Isaiah 52:5).

17.application: The watching world often forms its verdict on God from the lives of those who bear his name. Privilege — Scripture, sound doctrine, the teacher's chair — raises the stakes of hypocrisy rather than covering it.

25.exposition: Paul presses to the deepest badge of covenant identity. Circumcision is of value if you keep the law, but if you break the law your circumcision becomes uncircumcision. Conversely, an uncircumcised man who keeps the law's requirements is counted as circumcised, and stands as a living rebuke to the lawbreaker who has the written code and circumcision yet transgresses. For no one is a Jew who is merely one outwardly; a Jew is one inwardly, and circumcision is a matter of the heart, by the Spirit and not the letter — whose praise is not from man but from God. The chapter that began by dismantling the moralist's high ground ends by relativizing the supreme mark of Jewish privilege: membership in God's people is finally an inward, Spirit-wrought reality, not an outward sign.
25.original_language: The climactic contrast is en pneumati against grammati — circumcision "by the Spirit, not by the letter" (v.29) — the same letter-and-Spirit antithesis Paul will develop later (cf. 7:6). And the closing line turns on a pun buried in the name itself: Ioudaios ("Jew") derives from Judah, which means "praise" (epainos) — so the true Jew is precisely the one whose praise comes not from men but from God, the true "Judah" hidden from every human census.
25.historical_context: Circumcision was the foundational sign of covenant membership (Genesis 17:10–14) and the most visible, fiercely defended boundary between Jew and Gentile — Jews had died rather than abandon it under Antiochus IV. Paul does not abolish its significance but insists on its conditional character (v.25), an argument the Torah itself had made: Israel was commanded to "circumcise the foreskin of your heart" (Deuteronomy 10:16), and the prophets condemned those circumcised only in the flesh (Jeremiah 9:25–26). "Circumcision of the heart, by the Spirit" fulfills the promise of Deuteronomy 30:6.
25.christ: The circumcision of the heart, by the Spirit, is the new-covenant transformation promised in Deuteronomy 30:6 and Jeremiah 31:33, accomplished through Christ, who pours out the Spirit on his people (cf. 8:9). By locating true covenant membership in a Spirit-renewed heart rather than ethnic descent, Paul opens the people of God to all who are in Christ, Jew and Gentile alike (3:22). The badge that could not save points beyond itself to the inward work only the Spirit of Christ can do.

verses.note: Circumcision indeed is of value if you obey the law, but if you break the law your circumcision becomes uncircumcision. reformed Gill grants that circumcision was profitable — while it was in force, before its abrogation — but only on condition the law was kept. grammatical-historical Barnes notes it was the rite by which the relation to Abraham's covenant was recognized. To the transgressor the sign becomes worthless — a principle that reaches every outward rite and profession divorced from the reality it was meant to signify.

If a man who is uncircumcised keeps the precepts of the law, will not his uncircumcision be counted as circumcision? reformed Gill reads the words as spoken hypothetically — if such a Gentile could be found — and holds they are absolutely true only of those who from a principle of grace obey, in Christ, looking to him as the law's fulfilling end. The reality without the sign is accepted where the sign without the reality (v.25) is rejected; the privilege is leveled (v.11).

The physically uncircumcised who keeps the law will condemn you who have the written code and circumcision but break the law. reformed Gill notes the Gentiles are uncircumcised "by nature," circumcision being by command and art. grammatical-historical Barnes explains the judging: if the uncircumcised keep the law, their conduct condemns the Jew as a more upright man condemns us by his example. The obedient outsider judges the privileged insider who has letter and mark but no obedience (cf. Matthew 12:41).

For no one is a Jew who is merely one outwardly, nor is circumcision outward and physical. reformed Gill notes the apostle distinguishes between a Jew and a Jew — he is not the true one who is such only by name, nation, and profession. grammatical-historical Barnes adds that mere descent from Abraham, circumcised and externally conforming, never amounted to the character Israel's separation contemplated. The word anticipates the later verdict: "they are not all Israel who are of Israel" (9:6).

A Jew is one inwardly, and circumcision is of the heart, by the Spirit and not the letter; his praise is not from man but from God. reformed Gill describes the true Jew as one with an inward work of grace — not the law in the hand but in the heart, a worshiper of God in spirit and in truth. The heart's circumcision is the reality the outward sign foreshadowed (Deuteronomy 10:16; 30:6). And in the pun on Judah — "praise" — Paul names the approval that alone counts: not the applause of men who see the flesh, but the verdict of God who reads the heart.

witnesses.point: Circumcision profits only with the obedience it signified; to the lawbreaker the sign becomes uncircumcision, while the true Jew is one inwardly, marked by a heart circumcised by the Spirit rather than by the rite in the flesh.

Mere descent, the outward mark, and external conformity never amounted to the true character Israel's separation contemplated; the obedient Gentile, keeping the law's substance, condemns the privileged lawbreaker by his very example.

external.name: N.T. Wright
external.point: Reads circumcision as a covenant badge that Paul deliberately relativizes: the true people of God are marked not by the outward sign of ethnic Israel but by the Spirit-circumcised heart, so the "real Jew" is redefined around inward transformation — pointing ahead to the Spirit-led life of Romans 8 and a covenant family drawn from Jew and Gentile alike.

25.application: God reads a different résumé than the one the world checks. The mark he looks for cannot be inherited, performed, or displayed — only received, as the Spirit cuts away what the flesh could never reach.

_meta.title: God's Righteous and Impartial Judgment
reflection: Paul turns the reader's own verdict back on himself: "in passing judgment on another you condemn yourself, because you practice the very same things" (v.1). Where do you condemn in others what you quietly excuse in yourself?

God's kindness is "meant to lead you to repentance" (v.4), yet a hard heart "stores up wrath" under the same patience (v.5). How are you reading the patience you have been shown — as permission, or as a summons?

"A Jew is one inwardly, and circumcision is a matter of the heart, by the Spirit" (v.29). What outward badge — religious background, biblical knowledge, reputation — are you tempted to rest in, in place of the inward work of the Spirit?
